e_1.text:
The lower decks are different. Quieter. The constant background hum of the station's systems is muted here, replaced by an intermittent ticking — thermal expansion in the hull plates, where the station's skin meets the vacuum.

Your data chip projects a holographic waypoint arrow, guiding you deeper. The corridors here are wider than the maintenance shafts above, designed for cargo transport. But they are dark, and the walls are rimed with frost where the heating has failed.

After ten minutes of walking, the corridor forks. The waypoint arrow points left, toward what the station map labels 'Research Lab 7.' But from the right, you hear something unexpected — a voice. Human. Speaking in clipped, authoritative tones.

Imperial comms chatter. Malachar's advance team is already here.

The dark presses in. Down here, everything you know about fighting feels a little further away.

choices.text: The left corridor narrows ahead — beyond it the walls change, darker metal, no frost, no corrosion
Follow the waypoint toward Research Lab 7
Investigate the Imperial comms
Warn Voss and plan an ambush
Take the side corridor marked 'Engineering Section 4'
A flooded passage branches off to the left — ankle-deep liquid reflects your light

e_2.text:
The loading tunnel ends at another bulkhead, but this one is already open. Something — the stalker, probably — tore the door half off its track years ago. You squeeze through the gap.

Beyond, the lower decks stretch out in cold silence. Your data chip projects a waypoint arrow, guiding you deeper into the dark. After a few minutes, you hear something that stops you cold.

Imperial comms chatter. Clipped voices, echoing from a corridor to your right. Malachar's advance team is already here.

choices.text: Follow the waypoint toward the Void Compass
Investigate the Imperial comms
Take the side corridor marked 'Engineering Section 4'

e_eng_1.text:
The corridor widens into a vaulted space that must have been impressive once. The sign above the entrance reads ENGINEERING SECTION 4 — PROPULSION RESEARCH in faded stencil. Below it, someone scratched a later addition: DECOMMISSIONED — NO ENTRY BY ORDER OF STATION AUTHORITY.

Station Authority hasn't existed for three years. You duck under the sign.

The section opens into a long gallery with workbenches bolted to both walls. Most of the equipment is gone — stripped for parts long ago — but the benches themselves remain, scarred and stained by decades of use. Above them, cable runs sag from the ceiling like dead vines. The air is noticeably colder here, and each breath comes out in a faint cloud.

Ahead, the gallery continues into darkness. To your right, a heavy door stands ajar, marked DRIVE CORE TEST BAY. To your left, a narrow passage runs between conduit banks toward what looks like a maintenance junction.

choices.text: Enter the Drive Core Test Bay
Follow the passage toward the maintenance junction
Continue through the gallery toward the far end

e_eng_2.text:
The Drive Core Test Bay is a circular room dominated by a massive cradle in its centre — the mounting point for experimental drive cores during testing. The cradle is empty now, its clamps open like the fingers of a dead hand. Scorch marks radiate outward from it across the floor and up the walls. Whatever they tested last, it did not go well.

The walls are covered in instrumentation panels, all dead. But on a workbench near the door, you find something intact: a set of handwritten research notes, pinned under a broken calibration tool. The handwriting is precise, small, the kind that belongs to someone who writes reports for a living.

The notes describe test protocols for 'artefact-integrated propulsion' — attempts to use Precursor technology as a component in human drive systems. The results are clinical: 'Artefact refuses integration. Rejects all bonding agents. Surface temperature increases during contact with human-manufactured components. Recommend discontinuation of programme.'

The last entry is dated eleven years ago. Below it, in different handwriting, a single line: 'Bureau assumes control of all Precursor materials. Effective immediately.'

The Bureau. Malachar's people. They were here a decade ago, stripping the station of anything Precursor-related. The Void Compass was the one they missed.

choices.text: Search the bay more thoroughly
Return to the gallery

e_eng_notes.text:
You work your way around the test bay, opening panels and checking storage compartments. Most are empty — the Bureau was thorough. But in a sealed locker beneath the main console, you find a binder of handling protocols for Precursor materials.

The protocols are detailed and deeply cautious. They describe Precursor artefacts as 'non-responsive to electromagnetic analysis,' 'thermally anomalous,' and 'resistant to all forms of mechanical stress testing.' One section catches your eye: a procedure for Precursor chambers. Pressure plates, light-carrying channels, geometric triggers. The researchers had mapped the mechanisms. They could not understand them, but they could describe them.

You tear out the relevant pages and fold them into your jacket. The knowledge won't change what you find, but it might change how fast you recognise it.

choices.text: Return to the gallery

e_eng_3.text:
The gallery narrows as you move deeper into Engineering Section 4. The workbenches give way to heavy machinery — reactor test rigs, shielding assemblies, things bolted to the floor with industrial fasteners thicker than your arm. Everything is cold and dark and covered in a fine grey dust that no one has disturbed in years.

Then you hear it. A low mechanical whine, cycling up and down. Not station systems — too regular, too deliberate. Something is active ahead.

You round a corner and see it: a security drone, wedged in the junction where Engineering Section 4 meets the main lower deck corridor. It is an old model — Station Authority hardware, not Imperial — hovering half a metre off the ground, its sensor array sweeping back and forth. Its chassis is dented and one of its stabiliser fins is bent, but its weapons port is intact and tracking.

It has been here since before the station fell. Still running its patrol route. Still following orders from an authority that no longer exists.

choices.text: Try to slip past while its sensor sweeps away from you
Destroy it
Use the EMP grenade to disable it
Go back — find another route

e_eng_drone_luck.text: You time the sweep. The drone's sensor array rotates on a five-second cycle — three seconds scanning forward, two seconds returning. You wait for the return sweep and move.

test_luck.lucky_text: You cross the junction in the blind spot between sweeps, pressing yourself flat against the far wall. The drone's sensor passes over the space you occupied a second ago and finds nothing. You exhale and keep moving.
test_luck.unlucky_text: Your boot catches a cable on the floor. The drone's sensor snaps toward the vibration and locks on. A targeting laser paints your chest red. You throw yourself sideways as the first shot scorches the wall behind you. No choice now.

e_eng_drone_fight.text: The drone registers you as hostile and drops into combat mode. Its chassis tilts forward, weapons port deploying, and it fires. The shot goes wide — the bent stabiliser fin throws off its aim — but the next one won't miss.

e_eng_drone_emp.text:
You pull the pin on the EMP grenade and roll it across the floor toward the drone. The detonation is a sharp blue-white flash and a sound like a transformer exploding. The drone drops out of the air instantly, hitting the deck with a clang that echoes through the corridor. Its sensor array goes dark. Its weapons port locks mid-rotation.

The lights in this section die too — everything electronic within range is dead. You navigate by touch for twenty metres until the station's systems pick up again.

choices.text: Continue through the junction

e_eng_beyond.text:
Beyond the drone junction, the corridor connects to the main lower deck route. You are past Engineering Section 4 and closer to Research Lab 7. The waypoint arrow on your data chip adjusts, pointing ahead and slightly down.

From here, you can see the approaches to the lab. The corridor is wider, the ceiling higher — pre-Imperial architecture showing through the station's later modifications. The walls are smoother, the angles cleaner. You are entering the oldest part of the station.

Ahead, you notice a side passage with faded writing on the walls. To your right, the corridor floor looks wrong — the deck plates are discoloured. And from further on, you can hear boots.

choices.text: Head for Research Lab 7
Check the side passage with the writing
The deck plates ahead look wrong — proceed carefully
Imperial patrol ahead — assess the situation

e_eng_junction.text:
The maintenance junction is a cramped node where six conduit runs converge. A terminal is mounted on the wall — old but powered, its screen casting a faint green glow across the pipes and cables. Station infrastructure monitoring. Still running on backup power after all these years.

You tap the screen. It takes a moment to respond, then displays a schematic of the lower decks — crude, outdated, but functional. You can see the corridors you've been walking through, and ahead of you, marked in red, Research Lab 7.

The terminal also shows active power signatures. Most of the lower deck is dead, but three clusters of activity are lit up: the lab itself, a node near where you heard the Imperial comms, and a third signature you don't recognise — deeper, below the main deck level, pulsing in a slow rhythm that doesn't match any station system you know.

You study the schematic. There's a service route from here that bypasses the main corridor entirely, running through old coolant infrastructure directly to the lab approach.

choices.text: Take the service route to Research Lab 7
Return to the gallery and continue exploring

e_flood_1.text:
The passage slopes downward. After twenty metres the reason becomes clear: coolant. A burst pipe somewhere above has been leaking for years, and the lowest section of this corridor is flooded. The liquid is ankle-deep, dark, and slightly viscous — reactor coolant, non-toxic but unpleasant. It is cold enough to feel through your boots.

Conduit banks run along both walls above the waterline, their cables sagging and corroded. The ceiling is low here, forcing you to duck. Your light reflects off the dark surface of the coolant in a way that makes the corridor look longer than it is.

The passage continues ahead, the water deepening slightly. You can see a junction thirty metres on where the corridor branches. From somewhere ahead — hard to tell exactly where, with the acoustics distorted by the water — you hear a rhythmic dripping that isn't quite random enough to be natural.

choices.text: Wade through toward the junction
Go back — this route is too exposed

e_flood_2.text:
The water reaches mid-calf at the deepest point. It is achingly cold, and each step sends ripples ahead of you that make the reflections on the ceiling shift and slide. The dripping sound resolves as you get closer — it is coming from a cracked pipe above the junction, drops falling into the flooded corridor with metronomic regularity.

At the junction, the water shallows. To your left, a passage slopes upward and dries out — you can see station lighting ahead, the first you've seen in this section. To your right, the corridor continues at the same level, flooded, and ends at a sealed bulkhead with a faded sign: LOWER RESEARCH WING — AUTHORISED PERSONNEL ONLY.

The bulkhead has a keycard reader. Its indicator light blinks amber — powered but locked.

choices.text: Take the left passage — upward toward the light
Use the security keycard on the bulkhead
Try to force the bulkhead open

e_flood_force.text:
You brace yourself against the corridor wall and pull at the bulkhead. The mechanism is old and the water has corroded the lower track. The door shifts — a centimetre, two — then jams. You pull harder. Something gives, and the door lurches open with a grinding shriek that echoes through the flooded corridor.

The noise carries. Sound travels strangely through water and metal, and a shriek like that could be heard two sections away. You freeze and listen. Nothing comes back. But that doesn't mean nothing heard it.

Beyond the bulkhead, a short corridor slopes upward out of the flood zone and opens into a dry antechamber. This is a back entrance to the research wing.

choices.text: Enter the research wing

e_flood_exit.text:
You climb the passage, grateful to be out of the water. The floor dries under your boots and the temperature rises marginally — still cold, but not the bone-deep chill of the flooded section.

The passage connects to the main lower deck corridor. You recognise the intersection from the station schematics. Research Lab 7 is ahead, through the wider corridor with the pre-Imperial architecture. A half-open door to your left shows a room with projected maps on the wall — someone important is inside.

choices.text: Head for Research Lab 7
You hear voices ahead — Imperial soldiers
Look through the half-open door — an officer is inside

e_flood_lab.text:
The keycard reader chirps and the amber light turns green. The bulkhead disengages with a heavy clunk and slides open, water spilling through the gap. Beyond it, a short corridor slopes upward out of the flood zone and opens into a dry antechamber.

This is a back entrance to the research wing. The walls here are cleaner, better maintained — someone kept this section in working order long after the rest of the lower decks were abandoned. Research equipment lines the walls: sealed storage cases, environmental monitoring stations, a decontamination arch that flickers weakly as you pass through it.

Ahead, through a reinforced glass partition, you can see the circular chamber of Research Lab 7. The Void Compass is visible on its pedestal, pulsing with that slow silver light.

choices.text: Enter Research Lab 7
Search the antechamber first

e_lab_ante.text:
The antechamber was a staging area for researchers entering Lab 7. Lockers line one wall, most hanging open and empty. Environmental suits on hooks, cracked and faded. A clipboard on the workbench holds a yellowed form: PRECURSOR MATERIALS HANDLING — DAILY LOG.

The log entries span three months. The handwriting changes every few days — different researchers on rotation. Most entries are brief and clinical. 'No change in artefact luminescence.' 'Temperature variance within expected parameters.' 'Artefact non-responsive to all stimuli.'

The last entry is longer. Different tone. 'The compass responded today. First confirmed response in 14 months of observation. Dr. Vasquez was running a standard proximity scan when the silver veins brightened. She reported a sensation of directional awareness — a pull, she called it. Toward galactic north-east. The sensation lasted approximately four seconds before the artefact returned to baseline. Dr. Vasquez has been removed from the project per Bureau protocol 7.'

Removed. You notice the word. Not reassigned. Removed.

choices.text: Enter Research Lab 7

e_lab_approach.text:
The service route is a crawl through old coolant infrastructure — narrow, lightless, and full of pipes that radiate residual heat from systems that shut down years ago. You move on hands and knees in places, navigating by the schematic you memorised at the terminal.

After ten minutes you emerge through an access panel into a corridor you recognise from the station map. Research Lab 7 is ahead. You approached from a direction the Imperial patrols aren't covering — they set up their cordon along the main routes, not the service tunnels.

You can hear them, faintly. Boots on metal, comms chatter. But between you and the lab, the corridor is empty.

choices.text: Enter Research Lab 7

e_imp_disarray.text:
The corridors beyond the sensor relay are different now. You can hear it — the Imperial comms net has fractured. Fragments of confused transmissions echo from side passages. Soldiers calling in positions that don't match the terrain. Orders countermanded, then countermanded again. The false sensor data is doing its work.

You pass a junction and see two soldiers arguing over a portable display. One is jabbing at the screen, insisting this corridor should read as cleared. The other is looking at the actual corridor — dark, unmapped, nothing like what his display shows. Neither of them sees you.

The confusion won't last forever. But right now, Malachar's advance team is chasing ghosts.

choices.text: Move past them while they're distracted

e_9.text:
The soldier drops. His partner, back at the relay, raises his weapon — but hesitates when he sees his comrade down. He makes a split-second decision: he grabs the sensor relay and runs, disappearing down a side corridor.

He will report your position to Malachar. The clock just got shorter.

You check the fallen soldier quickly. He carries nothing useful — standard Imperial kit, all biometrically locked. But his comm unit crackles with a final transmission before going silent: 'All units, subject is armed and hostile. Sector seven. Converge.'

choices.text: Move quickly toward the Void Compass
Follow the fleeing soldier — stop him before he reports

e_8.text:
Voss circles around through a parallel corridor while you watch from cover. He moves with the practised silence of someone who spent years on covert operations. You see him reach the sensor relay, open a panel on its side, and make rapid adjustments.

The soldiers do not notice. Their helmet displays are focused outward, scanning for threats in the corridors ahead, not behind.

Voss slips back to your position two minutes later. 'Done,' he whispers. 'Their sensors will show the lower sections as fully scanned and clear. Malachar will redirect them to the upper decks. We have maybe an hour before someone realises the data is wrong.'

You move on together, leaving the soldiers scanning empty corridors.

choices.text: Press on toward the Void Compass
Watch the soldiers react to the false data

e_imp_pursuit.text:
You sprint after the fleeing soldier. He has a head start but he is carrying the sensor relay — a bulky piece of equipment that slows him down. You hear his boots ahead, the scrape of the relay against the walls as he takes corners too fast.

The corridor branches. Left — the sound is louder. You take the turn and see him twenty metres ahead, fumbling with his comm unit while running. He hasn't reported yet. The unit is dead — your fight must have damaged the local relay.

He sees you behind him and drops the sensor equipment. Now it is just a man running from another man in a dark corridor.

test_luck.lucky_text: You close the distance. The soldier stumbles on a loose deck plate and goes down hard. By the time he rolls over, you are standing above him. He raises his hands. You take his comm unit — dead, useless — and his sidearm. You leave him in the dark. He won't report anything without his equipment, and by the time he finds his way out, you will be long gone.
test_luck.unlucky_text: The soldier reaches a junction and ducks through a doorway you didn't see. You hear the door seal behind him — a lock engaging. He is gone, and in a few minutes he will find a working comm terminal and report everything. You have wasted time and gained nothing.

e_imp_patrol_2.text:
You hear them before you see them — boots on metal, the click of a weapon being safetied, low voices exchanging sector codes. A second Imperial patrol, moving along the corridor ahead of you. Two soldiers, same grey armour, same professional bearing. They are sweeping the approach to Research Lab 7.

They haven't seen you yet. The corridor has an alcove ten metres ahead — a recessed doorway to a sealed compartment. Cover, if you can reach it in time.

choices.text: Slip into the alcove and wait for them to pass
Attack before they spot you
The sabotaged sensors have them looking the wrong way — walk past openly
Have Voss draw them away

e_imp_standoff.text:
You reach the alcove and press yourself into the recessed doorway. The metal is cold against your back. You control your breathing — slow, shallow, through your nose. The frost from your breath would give you away if they looked closely.

The soldiers pass within two metres of you. You can see the scuff marks on their boots, the condensation on their helmet visors. One of them pauses. His sensor display pings — a contact, maybe. He checks. The display shows nothing. He moves on.

You wait. Their footsteps recede. Ten seconds. Twenty. The corridor is empty again.

Your heart is beating hard enough to feel in your teeth.

test_luck.lucky_text: They pass without incident. You wait another thirty seconds to be sure, then move. The route to Research Lab 7 is clear.
test_luck.unlucky_text: As you step out of the alcove, one of the soldiers glances back. Eye contact, half a second. He shouts a warning and both of them turn, weapons rising.

e_imp_patrol_fight.text: The soldier closest to you reacts fast — trained reflexes, weapon up and firing in under a second. The shot goes wide in the confined space but the next one won't. You close the distance and engage.

e_imp_patrol_bluff.text:
Their sensors show this sector as cleared. Their helmet displays confirm it — no contacts, no anomalies. According to every instrument they carry, you are not here.

You walk. Not running, not skulking. Walking, the way someone walks through a corridor they have every right to be in. The soldiers' helmet visors track movement automatically, but their displays override — cleared sector, no contacts. One of them glances in your direction. His eyes slide past you the way eyes slide past furniture.

Voss, walking beside you, does not breathe until they are twenty metres behind you. Then he lets out a slow, controlled exhale through his nose.

'That,' he says quietly, 'should not have worked.'

choices.text: Keep moving toward the lab

e_imp_voss_draw.text:
Voss reads the situation without needing to be told. He pulls a spent power cell from his belt and hurls it down a side corridor. The clatter echoes — metallic, sharp, convincing. Both soldiers' heads snap toward the sound.

'Contact — sector nine,' one of them says into his comm, already moving. His partner follows. They round the corner and disappear, chasing nothing.

Voss watches them go with an expression that might be contempt or might be pity. It is hard to tell with Voss.

choices.text: Move to Research Lab 7 while they're gone

e_imp_officer.text:
Through a half-open doorway, you see something that makes you stop. A room — an old station briefing room, repurposed. Maps of the lower decks are projected on the far wall, marked with Imperial sector codes and search patterns. Equipment cases line the floor. And standing with his back to you, speaking into a comm unit, is an officer.

Not a soldier. An officer. Bureau insignia on his collar, a datapad in his free hand. He is issuing orders — calmly, precisely, the way you'd read a shopping list. Sector assignments. Search parameters. Timetables. This is the man coordinating the advance team's sweep of the lower decks.

He has not seen you. The door is half-open and you are in shadow. You could listen. You could act. Or you could leave before he turns around.

choices.text: Listen to his orders — learn their search pattern
His datapad is on the table — cut the strap and take it
Leave quietly — not worth the risk

e_imp_eavesdrop.text: You press yourself against the wall beside the door and listen. The officer's voice is clear — he speaks with the flat precision of someone reading from a script.

test_luck.lucky_text:
'Squad two, redirect to sectors twelve through fifteen. Lab 7 approach is low priority — the sensor sweep shows it clear.' He pauses, listening to a reply. 'Understood. Inquisitor Malachar's ETA is forty-five minutes. He wants the perimeter secured before he boards.'

Forty-five minutes. That's your window. And they're redirecting away from the lab. You file the information and move, knowing exactly how much time you have.
test_luck.unlucky_text:
The officer pauses mid-sentence. His head tilts. He heard something — your breathing, a boot shifting on the deck, something. His hand moves to his sidearm.

'Who's there?'

You don't wait for him to finish the question. You move — fast — away from the door and down the corridor. Behind you, the officer is shouting into his comm. Your position is compromised.

e_imp_datapad.text:
The datapad is sitting on the edge of the table, its strap looped around the back of a chair. The officer is facing the wall projection, comm unit to his ear. His back is to you.

You ease the door open another centimetre. Reach through. The vibro-knife's molecular edge cuts through the strap without resistance — a clean separation, silent. You lift the datapad and pull back.

The officer keeps talking. He doesn't notice.

In the corridor, you thumb the datapad on. It's locked, but the display shows the current status screen — a map of the lower decks with patrol positions, search schedules, and a countdown timer labelled 'MALACHAR ARRIVAL.' The timer reads forty-two minutes.

You know where every patrol is. You know where they're going. And you know how long you have.

choices.text: Head for Research Lab 7 — you know exactly which route is clear

e_research_notes.text:
A side passage off the main lower deck corridor leads to a narrow room that might once have been a records office. Filing cabinets line the walls, most of them forced open and emptied. But the walls themselves are covered in something the Bureau's cleaners missed — or chose to ignore.

Research notes. Handwritten, pinned directly to the metal with magnetic clips. They are old — the paper is yellowed, the ink faded — and they predate the Bureau's takeover by at least a decade. Station researchers, the original team. People who studied the Precursor artefacts not for military advantage but out of curiosity.

The notes describe the artefacts' behaviour in clinical detail. 'Non-responsive to electrical stimulus.' 'Surface temperature consistently 2.3 degrees above ambient, regardless of external conditions.' 'Artefact appears to absorb specific wavelengths of light — analysis pending.' Beside each note, someone has drawn careful diagrams of the artefacts. The drawings are precise but the proportions are wrong — the angles don't resolve into shapes your mind can hold. You look at them and they seem to shift.

One note, near the bottom, is different. Written in a shakier hand, with no diagram. 'I held the compass today. It showed me a direction. I know where it wants to go. I am afraid I will always know.'

choices.text: Move on

e_deck_trap.text:
The corridor floor here is wrong. You notice it the way you notice anything on a scavenging run — not consciously, not with analysis. Your feet notice. The deck plates flex under your weight in a way they shouldn't.

You stop. Look down. The plate you're standing on is discoloured — a slightly different shade of grey from its neighbours. And the bolts holding it in place are newer than the rest. Someone replaced this plate. Recently enough that the corrosion hasn't matched.

Below you, through the seam, you can see darkness. A drop. The plate is a false floor — step on the wrong section and it will give way.

test_luck.lucky_text: You step carefully, testing each plate before committing your weight. The false section is three metres long. You pick your way across, one hand on the wall for balance, and reach solid decking on the far side. Past the trap, the corridor continues — warmer, drier. Precursor construction is visible in the walls now, smooth dark metal emerging from beneath layers of human-installed panelling. Research Lab 7 is close.
test_luck.unlucky_text: You shift your weight to test the next plate and the one under your back foot gives way. Your leg drops through the gap. You catch yourself on the edge, arms braced, your lower body dangling over a three-metre drop into a maintenance shaft. You haul yourself up, scraping your ribs on the broken plate edge, and roll onto solid decking. Your side aches and your hands are cut. But past the trap, the corridor continues — warmer, drier. Precursor construction shows through the walls. Research Lab 7 is close.

e_lab_side.text:
A door off the main lab chamber leads to a smaller room. This was a secondary research space — workbenches, sealed containers, instrument racks. And on the benches, arranged with museum-like care, a dozen Precursor artefacts.

All of them are dead.

You can see the difference immediately. The compass on its pedestal pulses with that slow silver light — alive, aware, responding. These objects are inert. Dark metal, complex geometry, the same alien craftsmanship. But no light. No warmth. No sense of presence.

Some are cracked. One has been cut in half — a clean bisection that must have taken industrial equipment. Another is scorched, its surface bubbled and distorted by extreme heat. The researchers tried everything. Electricity, force, fire. The artefacts endured all of it and gave nothing back.

The compass was the only one that still worked. The only one that still listened.

choices.text: Check if the Precursor disc responds to anything here
Return to the main lab

e_lab_disc.text:
You take the Precursor disc from your jacket and hold it near the dead artefacts. Nothing. The disc is as inert as they are — room temperature, silent, ordinary.

Then you turn toward the main lab, toward the compass on its pedestal, and the disc warms in your hand. Not much. A few degrees. But in this room full of dead things, even that faint response feels like a shout.

The raised lines on the disc's surface — the ones that converge toward a single edge — are pointing the same direction the compass pulses. The same direction. Always the same direction.

They were made to work together. You are sure of it in a way that bypasses thought. The disc is not a separate artefact. It is a component. A piece of something larger that includes the compass and the room below the antechamber and the signal from galactic north-east.

You put the disc away. It is warm against your ribs, a mirror to the compass's heat.

choices.text: Return to the main lab

e_10.text:
Research Lab 7 is a large, circular chamber — clearly pre-Imperial, built into the station's original structure. The walls are smooth dark metal, unmarked by the rust and decay that afflicts the rest of the lower decks. Precursor construction. This room was ancient before the station was built around it.

In the centre of the chamber, on a pedestal of black stone, sits the Void Compass.

It is smaller than you expected — a sphere of dark crystal no larger than your fist, veined with threads of silver light that pulse slowly, like a heartbeat. The light moves in patterns that hurt your eyes if you stare too long. This thing is old. Not centuries old. Millennia.

As you reach for it, Voss speaks sharply from behind you — or if Voss is not with you, a voice speaks from the chamber's entrance.

choices.text: Grab the Compass quickly
Wait — check for traps first
Let the data chip interface with the chamber
You have seen this mechanism before. You know where the trigger is.
Check the side room first — more artefacts on the workbenches

e_escape_service.text:
You remember the service route from the terminal schematic — a maintenance crawlway that runs parallel to the main corridor but two metres lower, beneath the deck plates. The access point is a floor hatch fifteen metres from the lab entrance. You find it by counting conduit junctions.

The hatch is stiff but opens. Below, the crawlway is dark and tight. You lower yourself in and pull the hatch closed above you. The sounds of pursuit — boots, shouted orders — are muffled now, filtered through the deck plating above your head.

You crawl. The space is barely wide enough for your shoulders, and the Compass presses against your chest with each forward motion. It is warm. It pulses steadily, unhurried, as if the concept of pursuit does not apply to it.

choices.text: Keep crawling toward the cargo bays

e_escape_service_2.text:
The crawlway runs for fifty metres before opening into a junction where three maintenance shafts converge. You can stand here, barely. Above you, through the deck plates, the sounds of the Imperial search are fading — they are sweeping the main corridors, not the infrastructure beneath them.

A vertical shaft leads upward. According to the schematic, it connects to the cargo bay level — a direct route that bypasses the corridors where the soldiers are searching.

test_luck.lucky_text: The shaft has maintenance rungs set into the wall. You climb quickly, the Compass a warm weight against your ribs. At the top, a hatch opens into a quiet section of the cargo bay. No soldiers. No patrols. You are through.
test_luck.unlucky_text: The shaft's maintenance rungs are corroded. Halfway up, one snaps under your weight. You catch yourself on the next rung, the impact jarring through your shoulders, and haul yourself up the remaining distance. At the top, you emerge into the cargo bay, scraped and winded but clear of the Imperial cordon.

e_14.text:
You sprint back through the corridors, the Void Compass clutched to your chest. Behind you, the sound of pursuit grows louder — boots on metal, shouted orders in Imperial military cadence.

The station responds to the alarm. Blast doors you passed through minutes ago begin cycling — closing, opening, closing again in a pattern that follows no logic you can parse. The corridor lighting shifts from the faint white of emergency systems to deep red, bathing everything in the colour of warning. An automated PA activates, cycling through Imperial military protocol in a flat synthetic voice: 'Security condition red. All sectors. Lockdown in progress.'

You round a corner and skid to a halt. Two Imperial soldiers stand in the corridor ahead, weapons drawn, blocking your retreat. Behind you, more are closing in.

choices.text: Fight through them
Use the EMP grenade to blind them
Voss — create a diversion!
The service crawlway — you saw the access hatch on the way in

e_alarm_atmo.text:
The station knows something is wrong. You feel it before you see it — a shift in air pressure, subtle, as though the lower decks just inhaled. Then the lighting changes. The emergency strips along the corridor ceiling switch from white to red, and for a moment the entire lower deck looks like the inside of something alive.

Blast doors begin to move. Not all of them — the station's automation is patchy, failing in sections — but enough to change the geography of the corridors around you. Routes that were open three minutes ago are closing. Others are opening for the first time in years, releasing air that smells of cold metal and machine oil.

The PA crackles. The automated voice is Imperial standard — flat, clipped, inhuman. 'Security condition red. All personnel report to assigned stations. Unauthorised movement in restricted sectors will be met with lethal force.'

The voice repeats. And repeats. It will keep repeating until someone with authority tells it to stop, and that person is Malachar.

choices.text: Move through the chaos — the changed routes might work in your favour

e_escape_alt.text:
You take the side corridor. It is narrower than the main route but clear — no soldiers, no sensor equipment. The lighting here is dead entirely, and you navigate by the wall under your hand and the faint red glow leaking from the main corridor behind you.

The corridor curves. The curve is wrong — too smooth, too deliberate. The walls here are Precursor construction again, the same dark metal you saw in the antechamber. The station was built around this, and this was here first.

The Compass responds. A pulse of warmth through your jacket, stronger than before. It knows where it is. It knows what these walls are.

choices.text: Follow the Precursor corridor

e_escape_alt_2.text:
The corridor ends at a vertical shaft. Precursor-built — the same smooth dark metal, the same absence of bolts or welds. The shaft goes up. There are no rungs, no ladder, but the walls have irregularities — shallow handholds, placed at intervals that suggest a climber with longer arms than yours.

You climb. It is not easy. The handholds are smooth and the spacing forces you to stretch for each one. But the metal is warm under your fingers, and the Compass pulses with each grip as if encouraging you.

test_luck.lucky_text: You climb cleanly, finding a rhythm in the alien spacing. At the top, the shaft opens into a maintenance space between decks. From here, a human-built corridor leads toward the cargo bays. You are above the Imperial cordon.
test_luck.unlucky_text: Your hand slips on a smooth section. You drop two metres before catching yourself, the impact wrenching your shoulder. You hang there for a moment, breathing hard, then force yourself upward. At the top, a maintenance space. A corridor toward the cargo bays. You made it, but the fall cost you.

e_13.text:
You seize the Void Compass. The moment it leaves the pedestal, the silver light flares brilliant white and the chamber shudders. A deep harmonic tone reverberates through the station's bones — felt more than heard.

The Compass is warm in your hand, pulsing with alien light. Whatever that tone was, it just announced your location to everyone on the lower decks.

Then the lights die. All of them. For three seconds you stand in absolute darkness. When the emergency lighting kicks in — blue, flickering — you hear the sound of boots. Many boots. Running toward you.

choices.text: The compass is showing you something — one moment
Run for the exit — the way you came in
Look for another way out of the lab
The Precursor corridor — you saw it curve behind the lab
